title: Fitting a plasterboard access panel in a tiled wall
subtitle: Cut an opening and install a hinged or push-to-open access panel in a tiled bathroom or kitchen wall to reach pipework or valves
excerpt: How to fit an access panel in a tiled plasterboard wall. Covers cutting the opening through tiles and plasterboard, fitting the panel frame, and installing a hinged or push-to-open door that tiles can be fitted to the front of.
sourceNotes: UK bathroom trade practice for concealed access panels. Standard access panel installation guides from Manthorpe, Passivent, and similar generic UK suppliers (referenced principles, not brand instructions).

glossaryTerms.definition: An access hatch fitted with a spring-loaded latch mechanism that holds the door closed flush with the surrounding wall. Pressing the door releases the latch and the door swings open on concealed hinges. Preferred over hinges with an exposed knob or handle in bathroom and kitchen wall tiles, where a visible handle breaks the tile pattern. The door face is left unfinished on the tiled version so matching tiles can be bonded to it.

content.text: An access panel in a tiled wall gives a removable, re-closable hatch over a stopcock, isolation valve, or inspection point. The approach is cutting the opening, fitting the panel frame into the plasterboard, and fixing a
flush with the surrounding wall. Tiles bond to the panel door face so the hatch disappears into the tile pattern when closed.

Size the panel to align with grout joints on all four sides. A panel that cuts through the middle of a tile requires an ugly cut and leaves a visible gap; lining up with joints keeps the tiled face coherent when the panel is closed. Mark the cut lines on the tiles with a felt-tip and check for studs or blocking with a stud detector before cutting.

Score the grout joints on all four sides with the utility knife to prevent the tile face from chipping when the jigsaw runs. Fit a diamond or tile-cutting blade to the jigsaw and cut through the tile and plasterboard on the marked lines. Work slowly at corners (the blade vibration cracks tiles if the cut speeds up at a corner. Lift the cut section out. Clean up the opening edges with the utility knife.

Fit the panel frame into the opening. Most proprietary access panels have a lip that sits over the face of the surrounding wall. Fix the frame with the screws provided, drawing the frame tight against the back of the surrounding plasterboard. On some frames, the plasterboard edge clips into a groove on the frame rather than screwing through. Check the frame is level before tightening.

Bond the tile or tiles to the face of the door using flexible tile adhesive) standard adhesive will crack when the door opens and closes. Grout the tiles to match the surrounding grout, running the grout up to but not into the gap around the door edge. Once set, test the push-to-open mechanism. The door should spring open with a firm push and sit flush when closed.

attrs.symptom: The panel door sits proud of the surrounding tiles when closed
attrs.cause: The frame is sitting forward of the plasterboard face, usually because it was not pulled tight before the fixing screws were tightened.
attrs.fix: Remove the door, loosen the frame screws, push the frame fully back, and re-tighten. Re-bed the tiles if they were already bonded.
